quizzes.title: W12 Quiz 1
quizzes.description: Judges authorship, dating, canonical authority, structure, and chronology

questions.question: What does Judges 11:26 suggest about the book composition?

options.text: A long time horizon, since it refers to about 300 years
That Judges was written before Moses
That the book covers only one week

questions.explanation: The 300-year reference implies significant distance from early events and supports later compilation.

questions.question: What do the no king in Israel refrains imply?

options.text: A perspective aware of monarchy or its need
That Judges is only about priests

questions.explanation: The repeated refrain suggests the author writes from a vantage point where kingship is known or desired as a remedy.

questions.question: Why is Judges 18:30 important for dating discussions?

options.text: It dates the book to the flood
It mentions captivity of the land

questions.explanation: The captivity reference may indicate later editorial updating or an exilic/captivity horizon.

questions.question: How does the lecture connect authorship and canon?

options.text: Only books signed by Moses count
Authority rests on Scripture being God-breathed, not knowing the human author

questions.explanation: The lecture emphasizes theopneustos and pasagraphe: Scripture authority does not depend on identifying every human author.

questions.question: What cycle is introduced in Judges 2?

options.text: Disobedience, divine anger, repentance or groaning, and salvation

questions.explanation: The lecture follows the recurring Judges cycle of sin, oppression, cry, and deliverance.

questions.question: Which sections function as concluding narrative units outside the main cycle?
questions.explanation: Micah/Dan and the Levite concubine narratives are treated as independent concluding units.

questions.question: What does judge often mean in Judges?

options.text: Deliverer or war leader more than courtroom adjudicator

questions.explanation: The lecture distinguishes the judges from modern legal judges, though Moses can serve as a contrast for adjudication.

questions.question: How are minor and major judges distinguished?

options.text: Minor judges receive brief notices; major judges get extended narratives

questions.explanation: The lecture notes the difference between brief mentions and fuller narratives such as Gideon.

questions.question: Why is Judges chronology difficult?

options.text: It uses many round numbers and does not align neatly with broader timelines such as 1 Kings 480 years
It has no numbers at all
It gives exact modern dates for every event

questions.explanation: The prevalence of round numbers and tension with other chronological claims suggests non-precision.

questions.question: What does the lecture infer about the compiler of Judges?

options.text: The compiler wrote only a neutral diary
The compiler selected and arranged traditions to communicate a theological message
The compiler ignored all earlier traditions

questions.explanation: Judges is presented as a curated and arranged work with a structured theological message.

quizzes.title: W12 Quiz 2
quizzes.description: Judges 1-2 as continuity with Joshua, Judah leadership, incomplete conquest, and Bokim

questions.question: How does Judges deliberately link itself to Joshua?

options.text: It opens with after the death of Joshua

questions.explanation: The opening formula echoes Joshua and signals continuity with the preceding narrative.

questions.question: What is the function of the after the death of formula?

options.text: It proves the next book is unrelated
It links major narrative blocks compositionally

questions.explanation: The lecture compares the formula to other transitions such as 2 Samuel 1:1.

questions.question: What leadership problem appears at the beginning of Judges?

options.text: Israel asks who will go up first
There are three kings at once

questions.explanation: Unlike Moses-to-Joshua succession, Judges opens with a leadership vacuum.

questions.question: Which tribe does God appoint to lead?
questions.explanation: God designates Judah to go up, framing Judah positively and foreshadowing later leadership themes.

questions.question: What complicates Judah positive portrayal?

options.text: Even Judah has incomplete conquest, with iron chariots cited

questions.explanation: Judah has real success but does not fully drive out all inhabitants.

questions.question: Why are Caleb and Othniel material mentioned?

options.text: They prove Judges is about Rome
They replace the need for Joshua
They show no connection to earlier books
Judges reuses Joshua-era material to strengthen Judah favorable framing

questions.explanation: The lecture notes reprise of Joshua 15 material, likely selected to reinforce Judah prominence.

questions.question: How is Benjamin portrayed in relation to Jerusalem?

options.text: Benjamin fails to dispossess the Jebusites, contrasting Judah success

questions.explanation: The contrast may anticipate later Benjamin/Saul and Judah/David tensions.

questions.question: What do the failures of Manasseh and Ephraim help set up?

options.text: Ongoing Canaanite presence, conflict, and idolatry

questions.explanation: The wider tribal failures establish the conditions for later trouble.

questions.question: What happens in Judges 2:1-5?

options.text: The messenger of the LORD rebukes covenant disobedience and warns of thorns and snares

questions.explanation: The messenger interprets the conquest failures theologically as covenant disobedience.

questions.question: Why is the place called Bokim?

options.text: Because the ark is hidden there

questions.explanation: Bokim is explained etiologically from the Hebrew root for weeping.

quizzes.title: W12 Quiz 3
quizzes.description: Judges 2 as theological framework, idolatry cycle, relapse, and transition to Samuel

questions.question: What does Judges 2:6-9 repeat?
questions.explanation: The repetition links Judges to Joshua and shows thematic recapitulation.

questions.question: What does the repeated Joshua death material suggest?

options.text: Judges is not arranged as strict modern chronology
The text is only a census

questions.explanation: The lecture argues the repetition is a theological and literary bridge rather than a simple chronological sequence.

questions.question: What central problem begins in Judges 2:10?

options.text: Israel has too many faithful kings
A new generation does not know the LORD or his works

questions.explanation: The new generation forgetting Yahweh becomes the root of the cycle.

questions.question: Which idolatry language becomes a refrain in Judges?

options.text: Israel did evil in the sight of the LORD and served Baal and Asherah

questions.explanation: The lecture highlights did evil in the sight of the LORD and service to Baal/Asherah as recurring language.

questions.question: How does God respond to Israel covenant unfaithfulness?

options.text: He gives or sells them into enemy hands so they cannot stand before enemies
He makes every enemy vanish forever
He appoints Pharaoh as high priest

questions.explanation: Israel defeat reverses Joshua success because of covenant disobedience.

questions.question: What happens after each judge dies?

options.text: Israel relapses, often worse than before

questions.explanation: The cycle grows bleak because deliverance does not produce lasting heart-level obedience.

questions.question: Which phrases connect Judges to Exodus and Deuteronomy language?

options.text: Blessed are the poor in spirit
In the beginning was the Word
Did not listen to my voice and turned aside quickly

questions.explanation: The lecture links Judges phrasing to covenant warnings and golden calf language.

questions.question: Why does the LORD leave remaining nations in the land?

options.text: Because the land was never promised
Because Judah asked for no land
Because Israel transgressed the covenant and the nations become part of judgment/testing

questions.explanation: The lecture reads remaining nations as a consequence of covenant breach and a testing context.

questions.question: What positive but limited theme appears with some judges?

options.text: The Spirit of the LORD comes upon them for deliverance
They all keep the law perfectly

questions.explanation: Spirit empowerment highlights the need for divinely empowered leadership even in the dark cycles.

questions.question: How does Judges transition toward Samuel?

options.text: No king in Israel, Ephraim links, and Samuel as a judge prepare for monarchy
It resolves kingship with Saul already ruling

questions.explanation: The lecture connects Judges to 1 Samuel through shared geography, themes, and Samuel role as judge.
